weather.tip: Overcast morning at 21.9°C with a steep 94% rain chance — carry an umbrella and add extra buffer time for your commute, since heavy showers tend to snarl Bengaluru traffic fast.

around.headline: Youth crushed to death after getting trapped in a cage-style lift in Bengaluru
around.summary: A young man died after being caught in an old collapsible-gate ('cage') lift, with a separate report describing a hotel worker killed after sticking his head out of a moving lift to check what was wrong. If you use an old-style lift with a manual gate, keep your entire body inside the car at all times, even when it stalls.
around.source: The Times of India / The Hindu / The Assam Tribune

around.headline: Bengaluru school student's death after collapsing in class prompts negligence allegations
around.summary: A month after a 12-year-old died after collapsing at school, his parents say a CCTV review shows staff delayed getting him medical help. If your child's school doesn't have a clear, fast protocol for medical emergencies, it's worth asking administrators directly what it is.

around.headline: BMTC clarifies UPI ticketing glitch, promises automatic refunds
around.summary: After some bus commuters paid via UPI but didn't get a ticket printed, BMTC has confirmed the failed transactions will be auto-refunded rather than requiring a manual claim. Keep your payment confirmation screenshot until the refund actually reflects, just in case.

around.headline: Crumbling bridge near Sandal Soap Factory Metro station flagged for safety concerns
around.summary: A deteriorating bridge close to the Sandal Soap Factory Metro station has raised safety concerns among commuters and residents in the area. If you regularly walk or drive that stretch, watch for barricades or diversions in the coming weeks.

around.headline: CBI arrests MD of Bengaluru PSU Karnataka Antibiotics and Pharmaceuticals Ltd over ₹5 lakh bribe
around.summary: The CBI has arrested the managing director of a Bengaluru-based public sector pharma unit for allegedly accepting a ₹5 lakh bribe. It's a reminder that bribery demands from any government-linked office can be reported directly to the CBI or the state Lokayukta rather than paid.

around.headline: US strikes on Iran escalate; India advises its seafarers to avoid Hormuz voyages
around.summary: As US strikes on Iran continue, the Indian government has asked its seafarers not to take voyages through the Strait of Hormuz, a key oil-shipping chokepoint. A prolonged standoff there tends to push up crude oil prices, which can eventually show up as higher fuel and cooking-gas costs at home.

events.name: Happy Streets, Whitefield (final edition)
events.note: The car-free street event returns to Whitefield for what's billed as its last edition there — worth catching if you've enjoyed it before, since it may not return to this stretch.

events.name: Ranga Shankara's global children's festival
events.note: A festival aimed at getting kids engaged beyond screens, with performances and activities — worth it for a screen-free outing with children.

events.name: Mahesh Kale's Abhangwari concert
events.note: A classical vocal concert of Marathi devotional abhangs by Mahesh Kale — a good pick for a classical-music evening.

events.name: Channapatna Craft Experience at NGMA Bengaluru
events.note: A hands-on showcase of Channapatna's traditional wooden-craft techniques at the Art & Craft Experience Centre — good if you want to see or try a heritage craft.

events.name: Nouvelle Vague live in Bengaluru
events.note: The world-touring French band known for bossa-nova covers of new-wave classics makes a Bengaluru stop — worth it for a different kind of gig night.

street_smart.title: Your building's old cage-style lift jerks to a stop between floors and the collapsible gate won't shut all the way. What do you do?
street_smart.based_on: Real incidents this week: a hotel worker in Bengaluru died after sticking his head out of a moving lift to check what was wrong, and a separate report describes a youth crushed to death after getting trapped in a cage-style lift (The Hindu, The Assam Tribune, The Times of India).
street_smart.situation: You're in an old collapsible-gate ('cage') lift in your building or a hotel. It jolts and stops between floors, and the outer gate hasn't clicked fully shut. No staff are immediately around. What do you do?

choices.text: Push your head or arm out through the gap in the gate to see what's causing the stop.
choices.feedback: This is the exact mechanism behind this week's fatal Bengaluru lift accidents: if the car jerks back into motion while any part of your body is outside the gate's plane, you can be crushed against the shaft wall or door frame. Whatever you're trying to check, do it with your body fully inside the car.

choices.text: Stay fully inside the car, press the alarm/intercom button, and call the building's security or lift-maintenance helpline while you wait.
choices.feedback: Correct. Staying inside removes any chance of being caught between the car and the shaft, and the alarm or a call gets trained staff moving on a fix — lift mechanisms are not something to troubleshoot yourself mid-shaft.

choices.text: Try to force the gate fully open yourself and step out to the nearest floor level.
choices.feedback: Forcing a gate on a lift that's stalled mid-shaft, rather than level with a floor, risks a fall through the gap or the car shifting while you're mid-exit. Wait for staff to bring it level and open it properly, even if it takes a few extra minutes.

choices.text: Repeatedly jab every floor button and the door-open/close buttons hoping it restarts.
choices.feedback: This rarely fixes a genuine mechanical stall and can occasionally cause the car to jolt unpredictably, which is the last thing you want with a gate that isn't fully shut. One press to alert the system is enough — after that, use the alarm and wait.

takeaways: Never let your head, arm, or any body part cross outside the door/gate plane of a moving or stalled lift — that's the exact mechanism behind this week's fatal Bengaluru lift accidents.
If a lift stalls or a gate won't shut, use the alarm/intercom and wait for trained maintenance staff rather than forcing the gate yourself.

Old cage-style/collapsible-gate lifts are more failure-prone than modern auto-door lifts — report a gate that doesn't close flush to building management immediately rather than treating it as normal wear.

quiz.q: BMTC confirmed this week that commuters hit by a temporary UPI ticketing glitch (paid but no ticket printed) would get what?
options: A free ride voucher for a week
Nothing, since it's a rare glitch
quiz.why: Digital ticketing systems log the transaction ID even when a receipt fails to print, which is what lets an operator reconcile and auto-refund failed payments without commuters having to file a claim.

quiz.q: A stampede this week during which major annual festival in Puri, Odisha, left one dead and around 100 injured?
quiz.why: Puri's Rath Yatra draws enormous crowds pulling three massive wooden chariots for the Jagannath Temple deities — the scale and density of the crowd is exactly what makes stampede risk a recurring safety concern at the event.

quiz.q: NTA declared NEET-UG 2026 results this week, with over 11.2 lakh candidates qualifying — what share of them were women?
quiz.why: NEET-UG is the single entrance exam for MBBS/BDS admissions across nearly all of India, so its qualifier demographics are often read as a proxy for gender trends in who's entering medical education.

quiz.q: Which constitutional body is responsible for conducting Lok Sabha and state assembly elections in India?
quiz.why: The Election Commission of India is an independent constitutional authority under Article 324 — distinct from UPSC, which conducts recruitment exams, and NITI Aayog, which is a policy think tank, not an election body.

quiz.q: Hydrogen fuel-cell trains, like the one being flagged off in Haryana, generate electricity onboard by combining hydrogen with which gas, emitting mainly water vapour as exhaust?
quiz.why: A fuel cell combines hydrogen with oxygen from the air in a controlled electrochemical reaction to produce electricity, with water vapour as the main byproduct — no combustion involved, unlike a diesel engine.
